title: Merchandise exports by continent of destination
subtitle: Figures correspond to the value of [merchandise exports](#dod:merchandise-exports) by continental destination as a share of [gross domestic product](#dod:gdp) (GDP). All partner countries are classified into continent groupings according to OWID's classification.
caption:
Merchandise exports by continent of destination, India, 1949 to 2014
Figures correspond to the value of merchandise exports by continental destination as a share of gross domestic product (GDP). All partner countries are classified into continent groupings according to OWID’s classification.

Across the period from 1949 to 2014, the stacked area chart displays the value of India's merchandise exports to various continents as a percentage of its gross domestic product. The vertical axis measures this share from 0% to 18%, while the horizontal axis tracks the timeline. The data shows a distinct structural shift in export destinations over the decades. In the initial years, specifically around 1949, the total export share hovered around 4%, with exports to Western Europe and Asia forming the primary components. Following a brief peak in the early 1950s, the aggregate share declined, remaining relatively flat and low, generally below 4%, through the 1960s and 1970s. A sustained upward trajectory begins around 1980, accelerating significantly after 1990. By 2014, the total share of exports to GDP reached approximately 16%. The blue area representing exports to Asia constitutes the largest single component in the later years, expanding from a negligible share in the 1950s to roughly 8% by 2014. Exports to Western Europe, shown in red, also show growth, rising to occupy a band between roughly 2% and 4% in the final decade. Smaller contributions are visible for North America, South America, Africa, Eastern Europe, and Oceania, which appear as thinner bands stacked above the dominant regions. The chart records a sharp, isolated spike in total exports around 2008, where the aggregate value briefly exceeded 18% before correcting. The visual pattern demonstrates a transition from a low-export economy with diversified but small shares to one where exports to Asia and Western Europe drive a significantly higher proportion of GDP.
